elements.sent-2411-001: Unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) swarm networks face severe challenges of communication network split (CNS) issues caused by massive damage in hostile environments.
elements.sent-2411-002: In this paper, we propose a new paradigm to restore network connectivity by repositioning remaining UAVs based on damage information within local topologies.
elements.sent-2411-003: Particularly, the locations of destroyed UAVs distributed in gaps between disconnected sub-nets are considered for recovery trajectory planning.
elements.sent-2411-004: Specifically, we construct the multi-hop differential sub-graph (MDSG) to represent local damage-varying topologies.
elements.sent-2411-005: Based on this, we develop two distinct algorithms to address CNS issues.
elements.sent-2411-006: The first approach leverages an artificial potential field algorithm to calculate the recovery velocities via MDSG, enabling simple deployment on low-intelligence UAVs.
elements.sent-2411-007: In the second approach, we design an MDSG-based graph convolution framework to find the recovery topology for high-intelligence swarms.
elements.sent-2411-008: As per the unique topology of MDSG, we propose a novel bipartite graph convolution operation, enhanced with a batch-processing mechanism to improve graph convolution efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-009: Simulation results show that the proposed algorithms expedite the recovery with significant margin while improving the spatial coverage and topology degree uniformity after recovery.
elements.sent-2411-010: Meanwhile, to overcome the limited capability of single UAVs, the UAV swarm composed of hundreds of UAVs has been developed to adapt to dangerous environments and conduct various complex tasks, such as rescue \cite{rescue , reconnaissance \cite{reconnaissance , target attack \cite{attack , etc. The UAV swarm usually forms an ad-hoc network for collaboration, where task and control information is transmitted between UAVs through wireless communication links.}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: However, the UAV swarm network (USNET) is susceptible as the UAVs risk hostile strikes and external destructions.
elements.sent-2411-012: The damage of massive UAVs can result in substantial communication performance degradation and even divide the USNET into isolated sub-nets, causing the communication network split (CNS) issue.
elements.sent-2411-013: Several pro-active resilient mechanisms were proposed to reduce the probability of CNS issues \cite{active1, active2, active3 , however, these strategies can not maintain the connectivity under massive damage scenarios.}
elements.sent-2411-014: Therefore, the USNET requires reactive resilient algorithms to reconstitute the connectivity after the CNS issue.
elements.sent-2411-015: The studies on resilient algorithms originated in wireless sensor networks (WSNs) under static scenarios \cite{wsn1, wsn2, wsn3, wsn4 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Subsequently, the mobility-based resilient algorithms were developed for mobile WSNs \cite{mv_cut1, mv_cut2, mv_cut3, mv_ms1, mv_wsn1, mv_wsn2, mv_wsn3, mv_opt1, mv_opt2, mv_opt3 , and were later extended for USNETs \cite{hero, sidr, mgc, demd .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: The mobility-based algorithms can be divided into three categories.
elements.sent-2411-018: The first category of algorithms recovered the connectivity by repositioning the remaining nodes at the locations of destructed critical nodes \cite{mv_cut1, mv_cut2, mv_cut3, mv_ms1 , so-called the critical-node-based algorithms.}
elements.sent-2411-019: The meeting-point-based algorithms as the second category aggregated the remaining toward a pre-defined point \cite{mv_wsn1, mv_wsn2, mv_wsn3, hero, sidr .}
elements.sent-2411-020: The third category, the optimization-based algorithms, formulated the CNS recovery as an optimization problem and generated the solutions of recovery trajectories or locations for the remaining nodes \cite{mv_opt1, mv_opt2, mv_opt3, mgc, demd .}
elements.sent-2411-021: However, there are still several challenges in solving the CNS issues with mobility-based resilient algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-022: Firstly, many existing algorithms could not restore the connectivity under massive damage scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-023: For example, algorithms in \cite{mv_cut1, mv_cut2, mv_cut3, mv_ms1, mv_wsn1, mv_wsn2 were designed to handle CNS issues caused by single or multiple UAV destructions.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Many heuristic algorithms \cite{mv_wsn3, mv_opt1, mv_opt2, mv_opt3, demd were divergent and thus could not ensure the connectivity, especially more than 50\% of UAVs were damaged.}
elements.sent-2411-025: The second challenge lies in the insufficient use of topology information.
elements.sent-2411-026: For example, the meeting-point-based algorithms \cite{mv_wsn1, mv_wsn2, mv_wsn3, hero disregarded the network topology completely, and the algorithms in \cite{mv_opt3, sidr, mgc considered only one-hop neighbors that resulted in a limited receptive field.}}
elements.sent-2411-027: Thirdly, many existing algorithms \cite{mv_opt1, mv_opt2, mv_opt3, sidr, mgc only focused on the remaining nodes but neglected the information of the damage itself.}
elements.sent-2411-028: It is worth noting that the network is connected before the damage, hence the locations of destructed nodes distributed in the gaps between the divided sub-nets can guide the recovery trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-029: Other algorithms \cite{mv_cut1, mv_cut2, mv_cut3, mv_ms1 considered destructed nodes but required prolonged execution time to determine the critical nodes.}
elements.sent-2411-030: In this paper, we propose to restore the connectivity of the swarm networks by repositioning the remaining UAVs based on the locations of destroyed UAVs within local topologies.
elements.sent-2411-031: Specifically, we introduce the multi-hop differential sub-graph (MDSG) to represent the local damage-varying topologies, where each node's receptive field is extended into its multi-hop neighbors to capture additional features from local topology.
elements.sent-2411-032: Based on this, we develop two approaches to generate solutions for CNS issues utilizing MDSG.
elements.sent-2411-033: The first approach employs an artificial potential field algorithm to calculate the recovery velocities through MDSG, enabling simple deployment on low-intelligence UAVs.
elements.sent-2411-034: For the second approach, we design an MDSG-based graph convolution framework to determine recovery topology for high-intelligence swarms.
elements.sent-2411-035: As per the unique topology of MDSG, we propose a novel bipartite graph convolution operation, combined with a batch-processing mechanism to enhance the graph convolution performance.
elements.sent-2411-036: Simulation results indicate that the proposed algorithms expedite the connectivity recovery process.
elements.sent-2411-037: We also introduce the spatial coverage and the degree distribution to evaluate network topology, and our approaches showed significant improvements in recovered topology enhancement.
elements.sent-2411-038: Our contributions in this paper are summarized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-039: We propose a new paradigm to reconstitute the connectivity of the swarm networks through damage information within local topologies.
elements.sent-2411-040: Based on MDSG, we develop an artificial potential field algorithm for simple deployment on low-intelligence UAVs.
elements.sent-2411-041: We also design an MDSG-based graph convolution framework for high-intelligence swarms, enhanced by a novel bipartite graph convolution operation and a batch-processing mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-042: We theoretically prove the convergence of the proposed algorithms, and simulation results show that the proposed algorithms can restore the connectivity more quickly while improving the spatial coverage and topology degree uniformity after recovery.
elements.sent-2411-043: The remainder of this paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-045: Section \uppercase\expandafter{\romannumeral3 forms the system model of the USNET graph and CNS issue.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Section \uppercase\expandafter{\romannumeral4 and section \uppercase\expandafter{\romannumeral5 introduce the proposed methods with the traditional algorithm and deep-learning algorithm, respectively, and experiment results are given in Section \uppercase\expandafter{\romannumeral6 .}}}

elements.sent-2411-048: Resilient recovery algorithms for USNET have become a critical area of research, particularly in scenarios where connectivity is disrupted due to massive node destructions in hostile environments \cite{survey .}
elements.sent-2411-049: An example of CNS issue was illustrated in Fig.\ref{cha a, where the USNET was divided into 7 sub-nets.}
elements.sent-2411-050: Various strategies have been developed to address the CNS challenges, focusing on reconnecting divided sub-nets within minimum recovery time.
elements.sent-2411-051: Considering the different motivations for aggregating the surviving nodes, we can divide the existing approaches into the critical-node-based, the meeting-point-based, and the optimization-based algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-052: The critical-node-based algorithms aim to rebuild connectivity by relocating the remaining nodes to the locations of destroyed critical nodes.
elements.sent-2411-053: For example, algorithms in \cite{mv_cut1, mv_cut2 identified the global cut-vertex nodes as the critical nodes and addressed the CNS issues in small networks.}
elements.sent-2411-054: Zhang et al.\cite{mv_cut3 extended the recovery algorithm to large-scale WSNs by employing a finite state machine, effectively reducing the complexity of identifying cut-vertex nodes.}
elements.sent-2411-055: Younis et al.\cite{mv_ms1 considered a clustered USNET and designated the master nodes as the critical nodes, eliminating the need for additional identification processes.}
elements.sent-2411-056: However, the cascaded motion under massive damage increases the total number and moving distances of the relocated nodes and will reduce the network's coverage ability \cite{mv_ms2 .}
elements.sent-2411-057: As a result, the critical-node-based methods have poor performance in massive damage scenarios of swarm networks.
elements.sent-2411-058: The meeting-point-based algorithms guide the remaining nodes to aggregate toward a pre-defined meeting point, which can ensure the restoration of connectivity.
elements.sent-2411-059: These algorithms have been widely applied in mobile WSNs \cite{mv_wsn1, mv_wsn2, mv_wsn3 , with the meeting point usually located at the network center.}
elements.sent-2411-060: However, in large-scale USNETs where nodes are distributed across vast spaces, the edge nodes may be situated far from the meeting point.
elements.sent-2411-061: As a result, the recovery time increases because the edge nodes, shown in the red boxes in Fig.\ref{cha b, must cover long distances to reach the meeting point.}
elements.sent-2411-062: To cope with this problem, Mi et al.\cite{hero proposed to define multiple meeting points, which reduces the average moving distance of edge nodes.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Chen et al.\cite{sidr developed a swarm intelligence-based damage-resilient mechanism that calculates the recovery path for each sub-network as a whole.}
elements.sent-2411-064: This approach allows the network to restore connectivity without losing its original links and achieves path tracking with low communication overhead.
elements.sent-2411-065: The optimization-based algorithms formulate the recovery issues as an optimization problem to find the optimal recovery paths for the remaining nodes \cite{mv_opt1, mv_opt2, mv_opt3 .}
elements.sent-2411-066: With the development of UAV intelligence, more advanced approaches integrate deep learning and graph-based algorithms to evolve the network topologies.
elements.sent-2411-067: Mou et al.\cite{mgc applied the graph convolution operation (GCO) to solve the CNS challenge for the first time, where the information aggregation capability of GCO enables the algorithm to achieve state-of-the-art performance.}
elements.sent-2411-068: However, due to the nature of graph convolution, which involves information aggregation between neighbors, nodes with higher degrees tend to dominate the convolution operation, attracting the remaining nodes towards them.
elements.sent-2411-069: This results in a serious over-aggregation in space, as shown in Fig.\ref{cha c.}
elements.sent-2411-070: The optimization problem formulated by the CNS issue is generally non-convex, meaning that a well-chosen starting point for iteration can lead to a better solution.
elements.sent-2411-071: Inspired by the critical-node-based algorithms, we notice that the original USNET is connected and the destructed nodes can help direct the recovery paths towards the gaps between sub-nets.
elements.sent-2411-072: In our previous work \cite{demd , we developed a damage-embedding module that maps the location information of destroyed nodes into the features of their remaining neighbors, which enhanced the performance of the GCO-based algorithm.}
elements.sent-2411-073: This paper aims to explore an efficient model for characterizing, extracting, and leveraging network damage information, thereby achieving fast connectivity restoration and a more robust recovered topology.
elements.sent-2411-074: This section first constructs a graph topology to represent the UAV swarm network (USNET) and then proposes the massive damage model.

elements.sent-2411-116: The artificial potential field (APF) algorithm is one of the classic methods for UAV path planning \cite{APF , which calculates the velocity or acceleration for the UAVs by simulating the virtual force field to reach the destination location.}
elements.sent-2411-117: Therefore, the performance of an APF algorithm is mainly affected by the design of the force field.
elements.sent-2411-118: Since the remaining UAVs are supposed to fill the gaps between sub-nets, the differential topology can guide the remaining UAVs toward the locations of destructed UAVs.
elements.sent-2411-119: In this section, we proposed a Multi-hop Differential Sub-Graph based Artificial Potential Field (MDSG-APF) algorithm, aiming to utilize the damage information in a simple but efficient way for CNS recovery.
elements.sent-2411-120: The section includes the construction of the force field based on MDSG, the APF-based solution for (P1), and the algorithm analysis on convergence and recovery time, as will be stated as follows.
elements.sent-2411-121: The MDSG $G_{d,r_i ^k$ of node $u_{r_i}$ contains the location information about its destructed $k$-hop neighbor UAVs, and each destructed neighbor $u_{d_j}\in \mathcal{U ^k_{D,r_i}$ provides a node-level gravitational force $\bm{f _{d,r_id_j}$ for $u_{r_i}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-122: This node-level force is directed by location $\bm{p _{r_i (t_0)$ to $\bm{p _{d_j (t_0)$, hence the resultant force $\bm{f _{d,r_i}$ composed of all destructed neighbors is summed as $\bm{f _{d,r_i =\sum\bm{f _{d,r_id_j}$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-123: In other word, let position vector $\bm{p _{d,r_i}$ denote the central location of MDSG $G^k_{d,r_i}$ as \begin{equation}p_{d,r_i} = \frac{1}{|U^k_{D,r_i}|}\cdot\mathop{\sum}\limits_{u_{d_j}\inU^k_{D,r_i}}p_{d_j}(t_0), \label{pd}\end{equation} we have the resultant force $\bm{f _{d,r_i}$ given by MDSG is directed from location $\bm{p _{d,r_i}$ to location $\bm{p _{r_i (t_0)$, so called the differential force.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-124: When $u_{r_i}$ locates near the boundary of its sub-net, the destructed neighbors in MDSG are mainly distributed in the gaps between sub-nets, hence the force $\bm{f _{d,r_i}$ points at the gap nearby as shown in Fig.\ref{field a.}}

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Network recovery process by the MDSG-APF algorithm solution.} \label{alg1} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \renewcommand{\algorithmicrequire}{Input:} \REQUIRE Initial time $t_0$, indexes $\mathcal{I}_R$ and $\mathcal{I}_D$, MDSG $G^k_{d,r_i}(t_0)$ for $u_{r_i}$, remained position matrix $\bm{X}_R(t_0)$ \renewcommand{\algorithmicensure}{Output:} \ENSURE A covered USNET, final recovery time $t_r$. \STATE Initialize time $t_l=t_0$, determine $N_S(t_0)$ \STATE Calculate $\bm{p}_{d,r_i}$ and $\bm{p}_a$ based on $G^k_{d,r_i}(t_0)$ \STATE Calculate $\alpha_{r_i}$ and $\bm{v}_{r_i}(t_0)$ \STATE Calculate solution $\hat{\bm{v}}_{r_i}(t_0)$ and $\hat{\bm{V}}_R(t_0)$ \WHILE{$N_S(t_l)>1$} \STATE $\hat{\bm{V}}_R(t_l)\leftarrow\hat{\bm{V}}_R(t_0)$ \STATE $\bm{X}_R(t_{l+1})\leftarrow\bm{X}_R(t_l)+\Delta t\cdot \hat{\bm{V}}_R(t_l)$ \STATE Update $l=l+1$ \STATE Determine new $N_S(t_l)$ \ENDWHILE \RETURN $t_r=t_l$ \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-171: The MDSG-APF achieves the recovery of split networks with low complexity and thus is more suitable for the USNET with low intelligence and limited computing resources.
elements.sent-2411-172: Let us assume that the UAVs in the USNET possess advanced intelligence, allowing for the application of more complex methods to address the CNS issue.
elements.sent-2411-173: Referring to the system model outlined in Section \uppercase\expandafter{\romannumeral3 , the USNET inherently forms a graph structure, with nodes representing UAVs and edges representing communication links.}
elements.sent-2411-174: Given that the graph convolution operation (GCO) adaptively aggregates node features and updates relationship weights, it is well-suited to effectively capture the critical information from the damaged nodes in the MDSG.
elements.sent-2411-175: In this section, we propose a Multi-hop Differential Sub-Graph based Graph Convolution (MDSG-GC) algorithm designed to achieve faster recovery by analyzing the relationships between the damaged and intact nodes.
elements.sent-2411-176: The subsequent subsections first present the novel bipartite graph convolution tailored for the unique topology of the MDSG.
elements.sent-2411-177: We then introduce the structure of the MDSG-GC algorithm, which provides the solution to problem (P1).
elements.sent-2411-178: The MDSGs capture the local neighboring relationships between the damaged UAVs and the remaining UAVs.
elements.sent-2411-179: However, addressing the CNS issue requires a coordinated effort from all remaining nodes.
elements.sent-2411-180: Since our recovery method aims to determine the trajectory based on the information from the damaged nodes, we constructed a unified multi-hop differential graph that integrates data from both the damaged and remaining UAVs, incorporating the relationships represented in the MDSGs.
elements.sent-2411-181: The MDSG for each remaining node represents local links with limited scope but single MDSG can not provide a solution for all remaining nodes.
elements.sent-2411-182: To address this, we need to integrate all these MDSGs into a United Graph, which encompasses the features of every UAV from the original USNET prior to the destruction, along with the multi-hop differential relationships on a global scale.
elements.sent-2411-183: Let the united multi-hop differential graph $G_d^k=(\mathcal{U _u,\mathcal{E _{d,u ^k)$ with a fixed index $\mathcal{I _u=\{\mathcal{I _R,\mathcal{I _D\}$ of all $N$ UAVs, where $k$ is the hop number, $i_a^v$ is the label of the virtual aggregation node, $\mathcal{U _u=\{\mathcal{U _R(t_0),\mathcal{U _D(t_0)\}$ is resorted by $\mathcal{I _u$, and $\mathcal{E _{d,u ^k$ denotes the multi-hop neighboring relations according to the MDSGs.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-184: Likewise, the edge $e_{d,r_id_j ^k\in \mathcal{E _{d,u ^k$ exists for every $u_{d_j}\in \mathcal{U _{d,r_i ^k$ with the MDSGs of each $u_{r_i}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-185: The position matrix of $G_d^k$ at time $t_0$ is represented as \begin{equation}X_{d}(t_0)=[p_{r_1}(t_0),...,p_{r_{N_R}}(t_0),p_{d_1}(t_0),...,p_{d_{N_D}}(t_0)]^\top\end{equation} where $\bm{p _{r_i (t_0)$ and $\bm{p _{d_j (t_0)$ denote the locations of the UAV $u_{r_i}$ and $u_{d_j}$ at time $t_0$, respectively, $p_a$ is the aggregation location given by (\ref{pa ).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-186: Define the adjacency matrix of the United Graph $G_d^k$ as $\bm{A _d^k=(a_{d,ij ^k)$, the value of each $a_{d,ij ^k$ relies on the edge set $\mathcal{E _{d,u ^k$, that is, $a_{d,ij ^k$ equals 1 if $u_i$ and $u_j$ are $k$-hop differential neighbors in MDSG.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-187: Note that $\mathcal{E _{d,u ^k$ contains only links between destructed nodes and remaining nodes, the United Graph $G_d^k$ is a bipartite graph with the adjacency matrix as \begin{equation}A_d^k =  0_{N_R} A_{d,r_id_j}^k A_{d,r_id_j}^{k\top} 0_{N_D} \end{equation} where $\bm{0 _n\in \mathbb{R ^{n\times n}$ denotes the zero matrix, $\bm{A _{d,r_id_j ^k$ denotes the $k$-hop differential links between $u_{r_i}$ and $u_{d_j}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-188: Due to the special topology of $G_d^k$, we improved a bipartite graph convolution operation for the $G_d^k$.
elements.sent-2411-189: We can get the Laplace matrix $\bm{L _d^k$ of the United Graph $G_d^k$ according to (\ref{degree ) and (\ref{laplace ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-190: The features of $G_d^k$ form a structure in non-Euclidean space, hence the features need to be mapped from the time domain into the frequency domain to achieve the convolution operation.
elements.sent-2411-191: The Fourier transform in the time domain is defined by the eigenvalue decomposition of the Laplace matrix \cite{ft , i.e., \begin{equation}L_d^k=U\LambdaU^\top\end{equation} where the eigenvalues $\lambda_j$ denote the frequency and the eigenvectors $\bm{u _j$ denote the Fourier modes, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-192: Regarding $\bm{x}$ as a signal in $G_d^k$, we can define the Fourier transform as $\breve{\bm{x ={\rm FT (\bm{x )=\bm{U ^\top\bm{x}$ and the inverse Fourier transform as $\bm{x ={\rm IFT (\breve{\bm{x )=\bm{U}\breve{\bm{x }$.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-193: Therefore, the convolution between $\bm{x}$ and the convolution kernel $g_\theta={\rm diag (\theta)$ parameterized by $\theta\in \mathbb{R ^{|\bm{x |}$ can be represented as \begin{equation}g_\theta\ast x=Ug_\thetaU^\topx \label{ftconv}\end{equation} where $\odot$ is the Hadamard product.}}}
elements.sent-2411-194: The complexity of the convolution kernel in (\ref{ftconv ) can be extremely high since the eigenvalue decomposition requires a $O(n^3)$ computation overhead on global information.}
elements.sent-2411-195: A typical approach to decrease the computation complexity is to approximate (\ref{ftconv ) by truncated Chebyshev polynomials of the first order according to \cite{gcn , i.e., \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} g_\theta\ast \bm{x} \approx\bm{U}(\mathop{\sum}\limits_{c=0}^1\beta_cT_c(\tilde{\bm{\Lambda}}))\bm{U}^\top\bm{x} =\mathop{\sum}\limits_{c=0}^1\beta_cT_c(\bm{U}\tilde{\bm{\Lambda}}\bm{U}^\top)\bm{x} =\beta_0T_0(\tilde{\bm{L}}_d^k)\bm{x}+\beta_1T_1(\tilde{\bm{L}}_d^k)\bm{x} =\beta_0\bm{x}+\beta_1(\bm{L}_d^k-\bm{I}_N)\bm{x} \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $T_c$ denotes the $c$-th item in the Chebyshev polynomials with $T_0(x)=1$ and $T_1(x)=x$, $\beta_c$ is the $c$-th Chebyshev coefficient, $\bm{I _N\in \mathbb{R ^{N\times N}$ is the identity matrix, and $\tilde{\bm{\Lambda _d^k=\frac{2 {\lambda_{max }\bm{\Lambda _d^k-\bm{I _N$ is scaled by the largest eigenvalue of $\bm{L _d^k$.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-196: Inspired by \cite{mgc , we define the hyper-parameter $\epsilon=-\beta_1>0$ and let $\beta_0 = \beta_1+1$, then we have the bipartite graph convolution operation for the United Graph $G_d^k$ as \begin{equation}g_\theta\ast x=(I_N-\epsilonL_d^k)x.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-197: Let the position matrix of $G_d^k$ be the feature matrix, and the output matrix after $L$-th iteration of bipartite graph convolution operation can be expressed as \begin{equation}\bm{\hat{X}}_d = g_\theta\ast g_\theta\ast \cdots \ast g_\theta\astX_d=(I_N-\epsilonL_d^k)^LX_d \label{g}\end{equation} This indicates that the bipartite graph convolution maintains the shape of the feature matrix as $\bm{X _d,\bm{\hat{X _d\in\mathbb{R ^{N\times 2}$ are isomorphic.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-198: Note that $\bm{X _d$ consists of the location vectors of UAVs, $\bm{\hat{X _d$ can also represent a position matrix.}}}
elements.sent-2411-199: By extracting the first $N_R$ location vectors from the convolution output $\bm{\hat{X _d$ as the target locations for each remaining UAV, the final position matrix of the Remained Graph $\hat{G _R$ can be expressed as \begin{equation}\bm{\hat{X}}_R=[\bm{I}_{N_R}\enspace\bm{0}_{N_D}]\bm{\hat{X}}_d=[\bm{\hat{p}}_{r_1},\bm{\hat{p}}_{r_2},...,\bm{\hat{p}}_{r_{N_R}}]^\top.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-200: The recovery process for the USNET involves each remaining UAV $u_{r_i}$ flying at full speed towards its target position $\bm{\hat{p _{r_i}$ and remaining stationary upon arrival.}}
elements.sent-2411-201: Consequently, the total recovery time depends on the maximum time required for the remaining drones to reach their respective target positions.
elements.sent-2411-202: Thus, problem (P1) can be reformulated as \begin{equation}({\rm P}2):\quad \mathop{\rm min}\limits_{\bm{\hat{X}}_R}\quad T_{rc}=\mathop{\rm max}\limits_{r_i\inI_R}\frac{\|\bm{\hat{p}}_{r_i}-p_{r_i}(t_0)\|}{v_{max}} \label{p2} \quad {\rm s.t.}\quad \hat{N}_S = 1 \label{c2}\end{equation} where $\hat{N _S$ is the number of sub-nets in $\hat{G _R$ based on the position matrix $\bm{\hat{X _R$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-203: Building on the solution to the CNS problem provided by the bipartite graph convolution, we present the overall architecture of MDSG-GC as shown in Fig., which comprises three components: the batch processing mechanism, the GCO backbone network, and the loss function design.

The framework includes three key components: a batch mechanism for input and output processing, a GCO backbone network for solution generation, and a joint loss function for training.} \label{framework}
elements.sent-2411-204: When constructing the MDSG $G_{d, r_i ^k$ and the United Graph $G_d^k$, the hop number $k$ significantly influences the final topology.}
elements.sent-2411-205: A larger $k$ provides nodes with a broader view of their neighboring topology, enabling the MDSG to capture more information from the original network.
elements.sent-2411-206: However, an excessively large $k$ can create a complex multi-hop differential relationship between too many damaged and remaining nodes, which can diminish the effectiveness of the damaged information in guiding localized recovery.
elements.sent-2411-207: Therefore, selecting an appropriate $k$ is crucial for the performance of the method.
elements.sent-2411-208: In our previous work \cite{demd , the choice of $k$ was primarily based on network size and corruption scale, which generally yielded statistically satisfactory results.}
elements.sent-2411-209: Nonetheless, this $k$ may not be optimal for all specific damage scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-210: Inspired by the batch processing used in graph convolutional networks \cite{gcn , we propose a mechanism to address (P2) by evaluating different $k$ values in parallel.}
elements.sent-2411-211: The essence of graph convolution batching lies in combining multiple graphs into a new, disconnected graph by stitching them diagonally.
elements.sent-2411-212: For different hop numbers $k = 1,2,..., K$, we can generate a set of united MDSGs $\{G_d^1, G_d^2,..., G_d^K\}$ and their adjacency matrices $\{\bm{A _d^1, \bm{A _d^2,..., \bm{A _d^K\}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-213: The input graph $G_{batch}$ is constructed by merging these united graphs with \begin{equation}X_{batch}=[\underbrace{X_d^\top,X_d^\top,...,X_d^\top}_K]^\top A_{batch}={\rm diag}(A_d^1, A_d^2,..., A_d^K)\end{equation} where $\bm{X _{batch}\in \mathbb{R ^{KN\times 2}$ denote the concatenated feature matrix, and the block-diagonal matrix $\bm{A _{batch}\in\mathbb{R ^{KN\times KN}$ denote the adjacency matrix of $G_{batch}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-214: The batch processing mechanism eliminates the need to pre-define a single $k$ by directly providing solutions for different $k$ values.

elements.sent-2411-256: To credibly demonstrate the advantages of the MDSG-based algorithm in CNS recovery, we conducted a series of comparative simulations and case study experiments\footnote{The source codes are available on https://github.com/lytxzt/Multi-hop-Differential-Topology-based-Algorithms-for-Resilient-Network-of-UAV-Swarm .}
elements.sent-2411-257: The setup and results of these experiments are stated as follows.
elements.sent-2411-258: The original USNET consisting of $N=200$ UAVs was randomly distributed in a $1000\times 1000{\rm m ^2$ two-dimension area.}
elements.sent-2411-259: Based on the analysis in \cite{mgc , we set the communication distance as $d_{tr =120{\rm m}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-260: The maximum value of UAV velocity was defined as $v_{max =10{\rm m/s}$ for ease of calculation, and each recovery step had a time interval $\Delta t=0.1{\rm s/step}$.}
elements.sent-2411-261: The damage scenarios were divided into cases with different numbers of destroyed UAVs where $N_D$ was set from 10 to 190, and each simulation of different $N_D$ was repeated 50 times randomly for the statistic results.
elements.sent-2411-262: For the hyper-parameters in the framework of MDSG-GC, the number of layers in the GCN is $L=8$, and the hidden dimension is $d_s=512$.
elements.sent-2411-263: The learning rate is set to 0.0001 with an Adam optimizer, and the dropout ratio is set to 0.1 to alleviate overfitting.
elements.sent-2411-264: Three metrics for performance evaluation were presented in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-265: The recovery time $T_{rc}$ is the most important metric for measuring CNS recovery methods.
elements.sent-2411-266: Moreover, we introduced the spatial-coverage ratio and cumulative degree distribution to measure the spatial dispersion and topology uniformity of the final recovered $\hat{G _R$, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-267: The spatial-coverage ratio represents the ratio of the area covered by $\hat{G _R$ after restoration to the area covered by the original USNET, hence a larger ratio indicates a more dispersed spatial distribution that mitigates the over-aggregation.}
elements.sent-2411-268: The cumulative degree distribution $P_d$ denotes the proportion of remaining nodes with a degree number not greater than $d$ versus the total number of nodes in $\hat{G _R$.}
elements.sent-2411-269: Obviously, a resilient network requires the distribution with more low-degree nodes for better anti-destruction ability.

elements.sent-2411-314: In this paper, we studied the CNS issue of the USNET under massive damage.
elements.sent-2411-315: Specifically, we constructed the MDSGs for the remaining nodes to represent the destruction information of multi-hop neighboring nodes.
elements.sent-2411-316: We also developed two algorithms to plan the recovery trajectories based on MDSG for USNETs with low and high intelligence respectively.
elements.sent-2411-317: For the USNETs with low intelligence, we designed the MDSG-APF algorithm to calculate the velocities for recovery.
elements.sent-2411-318: Meanwhile, for the USNETs with advanced intelligence, we proposed the MDSG-GC algorithm based on a batch-processing framework with a novel bipartite graph convolution operation to generate the final recovery positions for the remaining nodes.
elements.sent-2411-319: Statistical results demonstrated that the proposed MDSG-APF and MDSG-GC algorithms can reconstitute the connectivity within a shorter time with significant improvements in both spatial coverage ratio and topology degree distribution.
elements.sent-2411-320: The simulation results also showed that the batch-processing mechanism can enhance the performance of MDSG-GC while reducing the training time with the pre-trained model.. {\appendices.}
